speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Myra, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Myra Slotnick, and it is my pleasure to be introduce-- to be interviewing Edith Klar on June the 7th, 1996 in Las Vegas, Nevada. And this is tape number 1.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Myra, anytime you're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Edith Klar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edith Steiner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell that for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-E-I-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: September 16, 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today

speaker: interviewee
text: Today, I'm 58.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Edith, to your best recollection, can you describe your family life prior to the outbreak of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was 1937, as I said, when I was born. And there was a great deal of unrest in Austria at that time already. And my family grew quite concerned and felt that something had to be done, they had to move. But how were they going to move? They didn't know. They thought--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe Brussels would be better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in your family at that time? Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my mother, my mother Erna and my father [INAUDIBLE], Arthur. And they both just were very concerned because there was an impending war, many rumors, which turned out to be true.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father at that time did odd jobs. He sold lace and elastic and different, different wares.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no specific position. He did that in Austria. He had various jobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a seamstress. She did a lot of sewing for families. There was no specific place that she went. She would move around and do a great deal of that to earn a living to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in your extended family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My extended family was my cousin Gerta, my cousin Peter, my uncle Oscar and his wife, my aunt [INAUDIBLE]. There were several aunts and uncles that had-- that lived with us at some point.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did they speak, or languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: They spoke German and French, and we were in Austria. And my mother had heard of a gentleman who helped people, helped the Jews cross borders, various borders in Europe. And my mother decided to make-- my mother Erna decided to make contact with this person to see if possibly we could get to Brussels, Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a year old. And my mother decided something had to be done. It was becoming apparent that they couldn't live there anymore. So Erna, my mother, made contact with this gentleman. And my mother thought that Brussels might be better, a better life. And possibly we could be free there for the future. She went to see this gentleman and asked him would he take us across the border somehow. And he said, no, he refused her. He said he couldn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What constraints were on your family at that time, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the constraints were we did have a grandma, which is, which, who was my father's mother, paternal grandmother. And she was older, and there were a lot of things to be concerned about because it meant going on foot across the border. And so this gentleman at first, like I said, refused. And my mother pleaded and begged with him. And he finally gave in to do this for us. So my parents made preparations to do this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to pay him anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we paid him. He was paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was he paid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Money, German money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how much?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the amount, I don't know an amount. But this is something he did for a living. And he was a good Samaritan, he did this with his heart. But of course, you know, he did get paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot recall his name. And I don't think my parents can, either. It's been so long ago. We-- [SIGH] we prepared to go. We took nothing with us. As I said, I was just a year old. And there was grandma, who was just a little sickly, slow. He explained to us, came to us, explained to us how he was going to do this, that we could have nothing on our persons, that I had to be kept quiet, that silence and speed was of the utmost importance. And we would have to go through the forest. I feel as though I've lived this. I know there's no way that I could possibly have remembered this. But somehow, I just feel it. I-- I feel as-- I see it so many times. We took off one night in the middle of the night, mother Erna, my father Arthur, and my grandma. And we went, we stepped into the forest, pitch darkness, silence, and with our guide. And my grandma was very slow. But we had to walk. So my family had to walk so fast. And my mother was so hungry, she was so tired. She said she was so exhausted. It was just awful. And grandma couldn't keep up the speed, but they pushed her. And I cried, I cried so much. And my mother muffled my sounds as much as she could. And she gave me the bottle, whatever she could do to-- to quiet me down, sleep, whatever. And they had to move very quickly, hours and hours of walking in the cold. They finally got to the border safely. Grandma made it. We're hungry. And we settled-- we settled in an apartment along with aunts, uncles, cousins. It was a one bedroom. Actually, it was one big room with a small nook, a couple of beds. I don't know how we slept. I-- I really, I remember the room perfectly. It was one big room with a stove and a little room on the side with a couple of beds. But there were like at least 10 people in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: How did they do it? I really don't know. I just-- I knew I was there, I remember it very well. And there was grandma. It was always dark, always quiet. Silence was the most important thing, top priority.

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't speak, I couldn't play, I had no interaction. My only interaction was with my grandmother. She was just absolutely wonderful. She-- when I was hungry, I was hungry a lot. And she would give me a treat. She'd have little sugar cubes and she would say, Edie, she would say-- or [? Edith ?] in German-- she would say zucker, which is sugar in German. And I would come running for this treat. And that was the greatest thing she could do for me. She was wonderful. I was surrounded with a lot of love because maybe I was an inspiration to the family because I was little, you know, and they had something. They really had nothing in their lives, no food, no jobs, no hope. The only hope was death. I mean, ultimately, death. It was a very, very sad experience. My other grandmother, my mother's mother, was very ill at the time. And she was living with her daughter in an apartment, a very small apartment. The Gestapo came to the door, banged on the door, broke the door down, and yelled Jews, Jews, pulled her out of bed, from a sick bed, and took her outside and threw her in some kind of truck, whatever they used. The Germans took all bodies, live bodies. This is a neighbor that saw this. And her daughter wanted to go with moms. Let me go with mother, let me go with a cry. The Germans said no, you go somewhere else. Well, we found out from a tracing bureau after the war that these two were exterminated. So it was a heartbreaking thing for my mother, helpless. There's nothing we could do, you know, about it. Just at the onset of the war, my aunt and uncle, my father's sister and brother-in-law, came over to see my mother and father, Erna and Arthur, and said, Arthur, you know, I received this beautiful letter, wonderful letter. This is a chance for us to have a better life. Look at you, you have no job, you have no food, you've got nothing in this world. This is a letter, the government has sent, the German government has sent to us, and said they want to help us. They want us to have jobs. There's a beautiful place in the country where you can have work, where we can have food, shelter, everything we want, a bright future. It's a beautiful place. And my father said, I got this letter. He said, don't go. And she said, you're a fool. Look at-- look at you, you've got a child. There is nothing here, come with me. My father said, no. This is a ploy, it's trick, it's a trap. Don't do it. Please don't do it. They had an argument about it. Back and forth. Come with me. My parents said no, no. Can't do it. Just don't-- begging, begging. They went, they didn't listen to my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they were taken, obviously on a train to their death. This was their paradise. And we found out afterwards they were killed. That was their wonderful place, a beautiful place in the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a concentration camp. And they met their deaths. My father tried to contact them in the best way that was possible to do at that time without being discovered. No response. And he knew. He knew that they met-- that they met their doom.

speaker: interviewee
text: That period-- around that period of time, my father disappeared. We didn't know what happened to him. Was this from Vienna or Brussels? This was from Brussels. My father disappeared for 15 months. Well, shortly after he disappeared, we found out that the Belgian police arrested him on the street and pointed to him and said, you're a spy. My father couldn't understand. I'm a spy? He spoke German. The Belgians hated the Germans. And so they accused him of being a spy. So they sent him to a camp. And he was tortured there and starved and suffered a lot. He tried to escape once, but they caught him. And he went back. And then, he did it successfully the second time. He just showed up one day at our doorstep. It was the biggest surprise in the world. We were-- we were ecstatic. Where did you come from? To me, my dad disappeared and reappeared, you know. How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just-- I was about four, four and a half, something like that. It was-- it was a celebration in misery.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: He does not talk about it. He cannot. And he has requested that I do not ask him. He begged me not to ask him. And I do not ask him. I don't know if my mother knows. But whatever he did and whatever happened is too painful to-- to discuss. One night, I heard the most horrible thud-- and I still hear it-- in the middle of the night in this house that we were in, this apartment. And we couldn't fathom what happened, we all ran out the front door of the apartment. We looked down the stairs and there was my grandmother dead. She's very, she was a very-- this is my paternal grandmother that I was so close to. She was a very independent lady. And the family begged her that if she had to go to the bathroom in the middle of the night, wake us up and we will take you. Don't go downstairs by yourself. Everything was always so dark. And it had to be that way. She, being so independent, she just got up, went, and she fell down the landing. She thought-- she took a step and thought it was the staircase. But it was the landing. And you know, there I was staring down at her body. I-- I just couldn't believe it, like, you know, what else was going to happen. People were disappearing from my life right, left, and center. But I knew that it was a lot of sadness. But you know, I thought that was the norm. This is my life. I wasn't, you know, I knew there was a lot of crying. My mother cried a lot. She was very emotional during that time because she was just scared, you know, just she had so many fears, and rightfully so. And there was a lot of sadness. I know nobody ever smiled, nobody was ever happy. Only my grandma was just delightful, you know. I really thought she was the greatest and she was gone. My uncle Oscar was picked up from the street. That was my father Arthur's brother. And he was thrown in a camp, labor camp. He contracted typhus. And he was so hungry, they didn't feed him, they mistreated him, naturally. They-- he was so abused. And because he contracted this disease and it was so communicable, they sent him to a hospital. He begged for food, begged for something. Please give me something to eat. He had-- they gave him something, which was a big mistake. They meant no malice, the hospital itself. But when you've got typhus, you must not be fed for a period of time. It killed him immediately. His wife escaped to Switzerland, thinking this was neutral country. And there was his wife and a little boy of three years old, beautiful little boy. Well, she was mistaken, and it didn't work out that way. They picked her up from the street, put her on a train, she was gone, was killed. And a cousin of ours disappeared, a man. And we found out he was killed in a concentration-- in one of the concentration camps. He left a wife and a little boy. There was unrest, continuous unrest. We were hungry. We lived on potato soup, potato soup, potato soup, which, I know, you know, to me it was good, whatever was there, a bit of bread, here and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you spend all your hours in that apartment? What actually did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, there wasn't anything really I could do. I just did little girl things, you know. I think I had maybe had a little doll, if I did. And my father did some tutoring. He did quite a bit of tutoring. He helped me and tried to prepare me for school, if there was ever such a day, a bright day that would come that I could go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you read?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I couldn't at five, I couldn't. The family decided that we had to split up, and we moved to a street, my mother Erna, and Arthur my dad, and myself. We moved to a street called [INAUDIBLE] in Brussels, Belgium, the same town. We lived-- we took an apartment on the third floor. We lived across the street from a [? kaserne, ?] which is a barracks. In this case, it was a German barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was approximately 1942, approximately. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in hiding from 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were in hiding more or less from the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which, 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: It began when we fled to Brussels on this journey. And across the street from us was this, like I said, this [? kaserne. ?] And we thought we would settle there, which would be least suspecting of being Jews. We would be least suspecting, of being-- suspected of being Jews, because we thought, well, maybe they won't look for us right there on the third floor. And it was always so dark, so dark. I couldn't speak. I had to be silent all the time. I couldn't look out the window. I heard the soldiers across the street. They were shouting, just shouting so loudly. They were marching in with their boots. I hear boats in my dreams. I went to sleep with boots, just marching, marching, cursing. They were cursing all the time. And that's all I heard. And there was a cafe downstairs, which was like a bar. And the landlord owned this cafe. And after the soldiers cursed and marched and maneuvered all day, all night-- half the night, their night continue downstairs in this cafe. There, they would yell and laugh and scream and we thought any day somebody is going to come upstairs. Somebody is going to point to that third floor and say, Jews up there. We were in constant fear. I knew, I knew there was fear around me. I knew, I always felt apprehensive that something was going to happen. I couldn't conceive the real, the real concept. But I knew there was nervousness and there was fright all the time. My parents were always frightened. We always stayed dressed. We didn't wear nightwear. There was no such thing. Because there was no point in getting undressed when we knew we would be taken eventually. There was no doubt that we would be taken away one day. It was just a matter of time for our deaths. [COUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we wandered about our street. The [? kaserne ?] spanned about a block and a half. And we thought, is somebody going to point up there. The landlord-- does anybody know? We thought, well, maybe they don't.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what I heard-- discussions, my parents whispering, talking about what they were going to do. One day we heard a doorbell in the middle of the night. And we were silent, we waited, a lot of commotion, a lot of German talk, a lot of yelling. And we waited. And it was just right across the hall from us, right there across the door of our apartment. And then silence after a great deal of noise. We knew something happened. We thought it was our doorbell because everything was in such close proximity. We found out the next day that a Jewish family was taken out with a couple of children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know Jews lived next door?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were some Jews that lived next door. We knew that. But apparently, they found out. And of course we were next. The nightmare of this shouting-- to this day, I can't take shouting. I cannot take anybody-- I cannot take conflict at all because it just brings back a lot of bad memories. There was one day when my father was quite ill. He had taken some sort of a stomach virus. And my mother and I really didn't go out very much at all, rarely. This was one occasion where we said we've got to go take the bus and go and pick up something to help him. And this was on the other side of town. We boarded a bus. And of course we were just petrified. [SIGHS] All of a sudden after just maybe a couple of miles, the bus was stopped. And we said, oh my god, this is it. And I started trembling and my mother was shaking. And she said, quiet, quiet, don't say anything. Please don't say anything, nothing. And I started to cry. And she-- I remember her hand. It was just clammy and quivering so badly. And about eight German soldiers came in and they said, off the bus.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we thought, well, you know, this is the time, this is it. He lined us up, asked us to-- told us to line up. He ordered us. He walked by everybody, one man, stopped, looked at me. And I was just shaking and crying. And my mother kept pushing me and said, no, no, don't, please, please keep quiet, keep quiet.

speaker: interviewee
text: He looked at me. He looked at me right in the eyes as though I see him. And he studied me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Any Jews here? Any Jews here? No, no-- my mother-- no. He looked at my mother. He kept walking, up and down and up and down. I waited, it was quite some time. Then, OK, get on the bus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] it was a horrendous, horrendous emotional trauma. [SIGHS] We went and did what we had to do, came right home. There were occasions when my mother was stopped in the street and asked directions a couple of times. And a German soldier came up to her one particular incident. He said, where's such and such a place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother would answer in French and say, [SPEAKING FRENCH] Now, well, she heard this soldier walking away and he says, ugh, she speaks French. What's French? They speak French-- because it was in German that they had asked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were various incidents at that time. And it was getting so bad that my dad said to my mother, we've got to do something. And my mother Erna had heard of a convent on the outskirts of Brussels, a convent that housed Jewish children, that protected them.
